content.text: Schema Evolution & Type Handling in Python Data Pipelines

Data rarely stays still. In production systems, source schemas change constantly — columns get added, renamed, or removed; data types shift from integers to strings; nullable fields suddenly arrive with nulls. A pipeline that worked perfectly last month breaks today because an upstream team added a new column or changed a date format.
is the discipline of building pipelines that survive these changes gracefully, and
is the practice of ensuring every value flows through your pipeline with the correct, expected type at every stage.
refers to the ability of a data pipeline to detect, adapt to, and manage changes in the structure (shape) of data over time — without requiring a full rewrite or causing downstream failures.

refers to the explicit management of data types throughout a pipeline: detecting what type a value is, coercing it to the required type, validating it, and dealing gracefully with mismatches, nulls, and edge cases.
Together, they form the backbone of
— the layer between raw, unpredictable input data and clean, trusted output data.
is simply a description of the structure of data:
Which fields are required vs. optional
Constraints like min/max values, allowed patterns, or enum sets

In real production pipelines, data comes from sources you do not fully control: APIs, third-party vendors, databases owned by other teams, IoT sensors, or event streams. These sources evolve. When they do:
A new column in a source CSV that your pipeline doesn't expect can silently corrupt downstream aggregations
A vendor changing a price field from
(integer) can crash a pipeline that assumes string parsing
A date field that switches from
produces completely wrong date values if not caught
where an integer was expected causes a
several steps deep in the pipeline, far from where the bad data entered
Without deliberate schema evolution and type handling, pipelines are brittle — they fail unpredictably, silently corrupt data, or require constant manual intervention.
: Pipelines continue operating correctly even when upstream schemas change incrementally
: Type coercion and validation catch bad data early, at the point of ingestion
: Schema diffing makes it easy to detect and log what changed between pipeline runs
: Explicit schema contracts make pipelines easier to reason about and test
: Well-designed evolution strategies let you handle old and new schema versions simultaneously
: Schema-aware pipelines require more upfront design — defining schemas, writing validators, handling branching logic for schema versions
: Validation and type coercion at row level can be expensive at scale; vectorized approaches are needed for large datasets
: For simple, stable, internal pipelines, strict schema evolution machinery may be unnecessary overhead
: If every schema change is silently accommodated, schemas gradually drift into an inconsistent mess — backward compatibility must be balanced with schema hygiene
The entry point of a pipeline is where raw data arrives — as a CSV, JSON, Parquet file, API response, or database query result. At this layer:
happens (automatically or manually): what fields exist, and what are their observed types?
checks the inferred schema against a known, expected schema
converts values to their target types
sends invalid records to a dead-letter queue or error log rather than crashing the pipeline
A schema can be represented in several ways depending on the tool:
As an Avro or Protobuf schema (for streaming systems)
These are distinct operations often confused with each other:
— transforming a value from one type to another:
— asserting that a value already is the expected type, rejecting it if not:
assert isinstance(value, int), f"Expected int, got {type(value)}"
: coerce where the transformation is safe and expected, validate to catch values that cannot be coerced.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     RAW DATA SOURCE                             │
│         (CSV / JSON / API / DB / Parquet / Stream)              │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   SCHEMA INFERENCE LAYER                        │
│  - Detect field names                                           │
│  - Detect observed types (may be wrong/ambiguous)               │
│  - Compare against known/expected schema                        │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  SCHEMA DIFF & EVOLUTION CHECK                  │
│  - New columns added?       → Handle (include or ignore)        │
│  - Columns removed?         → Handle (default or error)         │
│  - Type changed?            → Attempt coercion or error         │
│  - Column renamed?          → Apply alias mapping               │
└──────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┘
           │ Compatible                            │ Incompatible
           ▼                                       ▼
┌──────────────────────┐               ┌───────────────────────────┐
│  TYPE COERCION LAYER │               │  ERROR / DEAD-LETTER QUEUE│
│  - Cast to target    │               │  - Log schema violation    │
│    types             │               │  - Alert on-call           │
│  - Handle nulls      │               │  - Skip or quarantine rows │
│  - Normalize formats │               └───────────────────────────┘
└──────────┬───────────┘
           │
           ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   VALIDATION LAYER                              │
│  - Field-level constraints (range, enum, regex, not-null)       │
│  - Row-level rules (cross-field consistency)                    │
│  - Dataset-level rules (row count, null %, value distribution)  │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TRANSFORMATION & BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER              │
│  - Operates on clean, typed, validated data                     │
│  - Can trust types — no defensive casting needed here           │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         OUTPUT / SINK                           │
│  - Database, Data Warehouse, Parquet, API                       │
│  - Schema registered at sink (Avro registry, DB DDL, etc.)      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
There are four canonical strategies, borrowed from API and database design:
New schema can read data written with the old schema. Old fields are retained; new fields have defaults.
to a sales table — old records simply have
Old schema can read data written with the new schema. New fields are ignored by old consumers.
is added by the source. Old pipeline versions just ignore it.
Both backward and forward compatible simultaneously. The most restrictive but most robust.
A field is renamed, removed, or fundamentally retyped. Requires coordinated migration — old and new pipeline versions cannot coexist.
Never rely solely on pandas or Python's implicit type inference. Define what you expect before data arrives.

from typing import TypedDict, Optional

class OrderRecord(TypedDict):
    order_id: int
    customer_id: int
    product_sku: str
    quantity: int
    unit_price: float
    order_date: str          # Will be parsed to datetime downstream
    discount_pct: Optional[float]
    is_returned: bool

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class OrderRecord:
    order_id: int
    customer_id: int
    product_sku: str
    quantity: int
    unit_price: float
    order_date: date
    discount_pct: Optional[float] = None
    is_returned: bool = False
enforces types at runtime, not just at type-check time. It also does automatic coercion — if

from pydantic import ValidationError
from typing import List, Tuple

def validate_batch(
    raw_records: List[dict],
) -> Tuple[List[OrderRecord], List[dict]]:
    """
    Returns (valid_records, failed_records).
    Failed records include the original data + the error message.
    """
    valid = []
    failed = []
    for raw in raw_records:
        try:
            valid.append(OrderRecord(**raw))
        except ValidationError as e:
            failed.append({"raw": raw, "errors": e.errors()})
    return valid, failed

valid_records, error_records = validate_batch(raw_data)
print(f"Valid: {len(valid_records)}, Invalid: {len(error_records)}")

# Log errors for inspection
for err in error_records:
    print(f"Failed record: {err['raw']}")
    print(f"  Errors: {err['errors']}")
Step 3: DataFrame Schema Validation with Pandera
For pandas-based pipelines processing large tables,
validates entire DataFrames against a schema — column by column, with statistical checks.

import pandera as pa
from pandera import Column, DataFrameSchema, Check
import pandas as pd

# Define schema
order_schema = DataFrameSchema(
    {
        "order_id":      Column(int,   Check.greater_than(0), nullable=False),
        "customer_id":   Column(int,   nullable=False),
        "product_sku":   Column(str,   Check.str_matches(r"^SKU-\d+$")),
        "quantity":      Column(int,   Check.in_range(1, 10_000)),
        "unit_price":    Column(float, Check.greater_than_or_equal_to(0.0)),
        "order_date":    Column(pa.DateTime, nullable=False),
        "discount_pct":  Column(float, Check.in_range(0.0, 1.0), nullable=True),
        "is_returned":   Column(bool,  nullable=False),
    },
    # Dataset-level checks
    checks=[
        Check(lambda df: df["quantity"].notna().mean() > 0.99,
              error="More than 1% of quantity values are null"),
    ],
    coerce=True,    # Attempt type coercion before validation
    strict=False,   # Allow extra columns not in the schema
)

# Validate
def load_and_validate(filepath: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = pd.read_csv(filepath, parse_dates=["order_date"])
    try:
        validated_df = order_schema.validate(df, lazy=True)  # collect all errors
        return validated_df
    except pa.errors.SchemaErrors as e:
        print("Schema validation failed:")
        print(e.failure_cases)  # DataFrame of all failures
        raise
Schema evolution with pandera — handling added columns:
Detecting what changed between the schema you expect and the schema you received is the core of schema evolution monitoring.
Step 5: Handling Nulls and Missing Values
Null handling is the most common source of type errors in pipelines. Every type coercion must account for

import math
from typing import Optional, TypeVar, Callable, Any

T = TypeVar("T")

def safe_cast(
    value: Any,
    cast_fn: Callable[[Any], T],
    default: Optional[T] = None,
    null_sentinels: set = frozenset({"", "null", "none", "n/a", "na", "nan"})
) -> Optional[T]:
    """
    Safely cast a value to a target type.
    Returns default if value is None, NaN, or a null sentinel string.
    """
    if value is None:
        return default
    if isinstance(value, float) and math.isnan(value):
        return default
    if isinstance(value, str) and value.strip().lower() in null_sentinels:
        return default
    try:
        return cast_fn(value)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return default


# Examples
safe_cast("42", int)           # → 42
safe_cast("N/A", int, 0)       # → 0
safe_cast(None, float, 0.0)    # → 0.0
safe_cast("nan", float)        # → None
safe_cast("3.14", float)       # → 3.14
safe_cast("bad", int, -1)      # → -1

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "quantity":   ["3", "N/A", None, "7", "null"],
    "unit_price": ["19.99", "nan", "5.50", None, "12.00"],
})

# Replace common null sentinels before casting
NULL_SENTINELS = ["N/A", "null", "NULL", "none", "None", "nan", "NaN", ""]

df.replace(NULL_SENTINELS, np.nan, inplace=True)

# Now cast — pd.to_numeric handles NaN gracefully
df["quantity"]   = pd.to_numeric(df["quantity"],   errors="coerce").astype("Int64")
df["unit_price"] = pd.to_numeric(df["unit_price"], errors="coerce")

print(df)
#   quantity  unit_price
# 0        3       19.99
# 1     <NA>         NaN
# 2     <NA>        5.50
# 3        7         NaN
# 4     <NA>       12.00
Step 6: Handling Date and Time Types
Date/time is the single most frequent type-handling problem in real pipelines.
Timezone handling — a major source of silent bugs:
Step 7: Evolving Schemas Over Time — Version Registry
For pipelines that must handle multiple versions of a schema (e.g., data from different time periods), a schema registry pattern is effective.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Dict, Optional, Any, Type

# Version 1 — original schema
class OrderV1(BaseModel):
    order_id: int
    customer_id: int
    product_sku: str
    quantity: int
    unit_price: float

# Version 2 — discount_pct added
class OrderV2(OrderV1):
    discount_pct: Optional[float] = None

# Version 3 — loyalty_tier added, discount_pct made required
class OrderV3(BaseModel):
    order_id: int
    customer_id: int
    product_sku: str
    quantity: int
    unit_price: float
    discount_pct: float = 0.0       # Now required with default
    loyalty_tier: str = "bronze"    # New field


# Registry maps version strings to schema classes
SCHEMA_REGISTRY: Dict[str, Type[BaseModel]] = {
    "v1": OrderV1,
    "v2": OrderV2,
    "v3": OrderV3,
}

def parse_with_version(data: dict, version: str = "v3") -> BaseModel:
    schema_class = SCHEMA_REGISTRY.get(version)
    if schema_class is None:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown schema version: {version}")
    return schema_class(**data)


# Records tagged with their schema version at ingestion time
record = {"schema_version": "v2", "order_id": 1, "customer_id": 42,
          "product_sku": "SKU-999", "quantity": 3, "unit_price": 19.99,
          "discount_pct": 0.1}

version = record.pop("schema_version", "v1")
parsed = parse_with_version(record, version)
Step 8: PyArrow Schemas for Columnar Data
When working with Parquet files or Arrow-based tools (like DuckDB, Polars, or PyArrow directly), schema enforcement at the Arrow level is the most efficient approach.

import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import pandas as pd

# Define the canonical schema
order_schema = pa.schema([
    pa.field("order_id",     pa.int64(),   nullable=False),
    pa.field("customer_id",  pa.int64(),   nullable=False),
    pa.field("product_sku",  pa.string(),  nullable=False),
    pa.field("quantity",     pa.int32(),   nullable=False),
    pa.field("unit_price",   pa.float64(), nullable=False),
    pa.field("order_date",   pa.date32(),  nullable=False),
    pa.field("discount_pct", pa.float64(), nullable=True),
    pa.field("is_returned",  pa.bool_(),   nullable=False),
])

def read_parquet_with_schema(filepath: str) -> pa.Table:
    """Read parquet and cast to known schema, handling evolution."""
    table = pq.read_table(filepath)
    actual_schema = table.schema

    # Handle added columns (ignore them)
    # Handle missing columns (add nulls if nullable)
    aligned_arrays = []
    aligned_fields = []

    for field in order_schema:
        if field.name in actual_schema.names:
            col_idx = actual_schema.get_field_index(field.name)
            col = table.column(col_idx)
            # Cast to expected type
            aligned_arrays.append(col.cast(field.type, safe=False))
        else:
            if field.nullable:
                # Missing nullable column — fill with nulls
                null_array = pa.array([None] * len(table), type=field.type)
                aligned_arrays.append(null_array)
                print(f"⚠️  Missing column '{field.name}' filled with nulls")
            else:
                raise ValueError(f"Required column '{field.name}' missing from source")
        aligned_fields.append(field)

    return pa.table(
        {f.name: arr for f, arr in zip(aligned_fields, aligned_arrays)},
        schema=pa.schema(aligned_fields)
    )

Consider a pipeline ingesting daily order CSVs from three different vendor systems. Each vendor has a slightly different schema and date format. The pipeline must normalize all three into a unified schema, validate data quality, and load into a data warehouse.

import pandas as pd
import pandera as pa
from pandera import Column, DataFrameSchema, Check
from pydantic import BaseModel, validator
from typing import Optional, List, Tuple
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# ─── Canonical Output Schema ────────────────────────────────────────────────

canonical_schema = DataFrameSchema({
    "order_id":     Column(str,   nullable=False),
    "customer_id":  Column(str,   nullable=False),
    "sku":          Column(str,   Check.str_matches(r"^SKU-\d+")),
    "quantity":     Column(int,   Check.in_range(1, 50_000)),
    "unit_price":   Column(float, Check.greater_than_or_equal_to(0.0)),
    "order_date":   Column(pa.DateTime),
    "vendor":       Column(str,   Check.isin(["vendorA", "vendorB", "vendorC"])),
}, coerce=True)


# ─── Vendor-Specific Normalizers ─────────────────────────────────────────────

class VendorNormalizer:
    """Base class — each vendor subclass handles its own quirks."""
    vendor_name: str
    date_format: str
    column_map: dict   # {source_col: canonical_col}

    def normalize(self, df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
        # Rename columns to canonical names
        df = df.rename(columns=self.column_map)
        # Parse dates
        df["order_date"] = pd.to_datetime(
            df["order_date"], format=self.date_format, errors="coerce"
        )
        # Add vendor tag
        df["vendor"] = self.vendor_name
        # Cast numeric fields
        df["quantity"] = pd.to_numeric(df["quantity"], errors="coerce")
        df["unit_price"] = pd.to_numeric(df["unit_price"], errors="coerce")
        # Ensure order_id and customer_id are strings
        df["order_id"] = df["order_id"].astype(str)
        df["customer_id"] = df["customer_id"].astype(str)
        return df


class VendorANormalizer(VendorNormalizer):
    vendor_name = "vendorA"
    date_format = "%Y-%m-%d"
    column_map = {
        "id": "order_id", "cust": "customer_id",
        "item_code": "sku", "qty": "quantity",
        "price": "unit_price", "date": "order_date",
    }


class VendorBNormalizer(VendorNormalizer):
    vendor_name = "vendorB"
    date_format = "%m/%d/%Y"         # American format
    column_map = {
        "OrderID": "order_id", "CustomerID": "customer_id",
        "ProductSKU": "sku", "Quantity": "quantity",
        "UnitPrice": "unit_price", "OrderDate": "order_date",
    }


class VendorCNormalizer(VendorNormalizer):
    vendor_name = "vendorC"
    date_format = "%d-%m-%Y"         # European format
    column_map = {
        "ord_id": "order_id", "cus_id": "customer_id",
        "prod_sku": "sku", "units": "quantity",
        "price_per_unit": "unit_price", "ord_date": "order_date",
    }


NORMALIZERS = {
    "vendorA": VendorANormalizer(),
    "vendorB": VendorBNormalizer(),
    "vendorC": VendorCNormalizer(),
}


# ─── Schema Evolution Check ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

EXPECTED_COLUMNS = {
    "vendorA": {"id", "cust", "item_code", "qty", "price", "date"},
    "vendorB": {"OrderID", "CustomerID", "ProductSKU", "Quantity", "UnitPrice", "OrderDate"},
    "vendorC": {"ord_id", "cus_id", "prod_sku", "units", "price_per_unit", "ord_date"},
}

def check_schema_evolution(df: pd.DataFrame, vendor: str) -> None:
    actual = set(df.columns)
    expected = EXPECTED_COLUMNS[vendor]
    added = actual - expected
    removed = expected - actual
    if added:
        logger.warning(f"[{vendor}] New columns detected (ignoring): {added}")
    if removed:
        logger.error(f"[{vendor}] MISSING required columns: {removed}")
        raise ValueError(f"Schema break for {vendor}: missing {removed}")


# ─── Main Pipeline ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def run_pipeline(vendor_files: dict) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    vendor_files: {"vendorA": "path/to/a.csv", "vendorB": "...", ...}
    Returns a clean, validated, unified DataFrame.
    """
    all_frames = []

    for vendor, filepath in vendor_files.items():
        logger.info(f"Processing {vendor}: {filepath}")
        df = pd.read_csv(filepath, dtype=str)  # Read everything as string first

        # Schema evolution check
        check_schema_evolution(df, vendor)

        # Normalize to canonical schema
        normalizer = NORMALIZERS[vendor]
        df_norm = normalizer.normalize(df)

        # Keep only canonical columns
        canonical_cols = ["order_id", "customer_id", "sku", "quantity",
                          "unit_price", "order_date", "vendor"]
        df_norm = df_norm[canonical_cols]

        all_frames.append(df_norm)

    # Combine
    combined = pd.concat(all_frames, ignore_index=True)

    # Remove rows with null order_date (parse failure)
    null_dates = combined["order_date"].isna().sum()
    if null_dates > 0:
        logger.warning(f"Dropping {null_dates} rows with unparseable dates")
        combined = combined.dropna(subset=["order_date"])

    # Validate against canonical schema
    validated = canonical_schema.validate(combined, lazy=True)

    logger.info(f"Pipeline complete. {len(validated)} valid records.")
    return validated


# Run
result = run_pipeline({
    "vendorA": "data/vendor_a_orders.csv",
    "vendorB": "data/vendor_b_orders.csv",
    "vendorC": "data/vendor_c_orders.csv",
})
Reading all columns as their inferred dtype without validation.
for mixed-type columns and silently lets bad data through. Always read as
is a float. Pandas treats them interchangeably in most operations, but code like
for null checks in pandas contexts.
for columns that might have nulls.
. A single null forces the entire column to
silently. Always check what percentage of values became
after coercion — a sudden spike indicates a schema change.
Assuming UTC when no timezone is present.

Naive datetimes are timezone-unaware. If your source is in a different timezone than your warehouse, naive datetime handling will cause time-shifted data. Always store and process timestamps in UTC explicitly.
When a new column arrives silently and your pipeline ignores it, you lose visibility. Always log added/removed columns even if the pipeline doesn't fail on them.
When upstream renames a column, hardcoded column names cause
at runtime. Define column mappings as configuration, not scattered through transformation logic.
Read raw data as strings first.
When reading CSVs or JSON, ingest all fields as
initially. This prevents pandas from making incorrect type inferences (e.g., inferring
, dropping a leading zero ZIP code). Coerce types explicitly afterwards.
Fail fast on required schema changes, accommodate optional ones.

Missing required columns should immediately raise an error and halt the pipeline. Extra unexpected columns should be logged as warnings but allowed to pass (forward compatibility). This is the safest default.

null_rate = df["quantity"].isna().mean()
assert null_rate < 0.01, f"Coercion failure rate too high: {null_rate:.1%}"
Centralize type coercion in a single transformation step.
Never do type coercion inside business logic functions. All coercion happens at the ingestion boundary — by the time data reaches transformation functions, types are guaranteed.
Version your schemas and store them.

Save the expected schema alongside each pipeline run — store it in a schema registry, a database table, or a JSON file in the pipeline's metadata directory. This lets you replay historical data against the schema that was valid at that time.
Extra columns in the source do not cause validation to fail — only the columns you define are checked. This allows upstream systems to add columns without breaking downstream pipelines.
Different source systems use different null representations. Maintain a per-source map of null sentinel values and apply it consistently at ingestion.
For large DataFrames, row-level Python loops for type coercion are extremely slow. Always prefer vectorized operations:

# SLOW — Python loop, row by row
df["quantity"] = df["quantity"].apply(lambda x: int(x) if x else 0)

# FAST — vectorized NumPy/pandas operation
df["quantity"] = pd.to_numeric(df["quantity"], errors="coerce").fillna(0).astype(int)
For datasets that exceed memory, use

chunk_size = 100_000
validated_chunks = []

for chunk in pd.read_csv("large_file.csv", chunksize=chunk_size, dtype=str):
    chunk = normalize_types(chunk)
    chunk = canonical_schema.validate(chunk, lazy=True)
    validated_chunks.append(chunk)

result = pd.concat(validated_chunks, ignore_index=True)
For very large-scale pipelines (billions of rows), use
instead of pandas — it has built-in schema enforcement, lazy evaluation, and is 5–20x faster than pandas for most type operations:

import polars as pl

schema = {
    "order_id":   pl.Int64,
    "quantity":   pl.Int32,
    "unit_price": pl.Float64,
    "order_date": pl.Date,
}

df = pl.read_csv("orders.csv", schema=schema, try_parse_dates=True)
Large-scale DQ with suites, docs, and alerting
REST API schemas, complex nested objects
Schema validation against JSON Schema standard
Central schema storage and version management
SQL-level schema tests in data warehouse

: If pipeline outputs are used in SQL queries, a value coerced from string to int that contains SQL fragments could be dangerous. Always use parameterized queries — never string-format SQL with pipeline data, regardless of how confident you are in type validation.
: Pydantic validators and pandera checks can be bypassed with
(pydantic) or by directly constructing DataFrames. Never use these bypass patterns in production ingestion code.
: When pandas coerces bad values to
, data is silently dropped. For financial or compliance data, failing loudly on bad data is safer than silently discarding it. Consider
only for fields where nulls are acceptable.

: If a pipeline is rerun on old data (backfill), applying the current schema to historical data with an older format will fail or corrupt. Always store the schema version with the data and use versioned schema classes for reprocessing.
silently overflows in NumPy. Always validate value ranges before downcasting:

assert df["quantity"].max() <= 2_147_483_647, "int32 overflow risk"
df["quantity"] = df["quantity"].astype("int32")
Define schemas explicitly before data arrives
— never rely on inference alone; schemas are contracts between data producers and consumers
— coerce at the ingestion boundary, validate immediately after, then trust types throughout the rest of the pipeline
— null sentinels vary by source; a comprehensive
utility and null sentinel map prevents entire categories of runtime errors
Detect schema diffs, don't just tolerate them
— log every added, removed, or retyped column even if the pipeline continues; invisible schema drift compounds over time into a maintenance crisis
— these two libraries cover the vast majority of production Python pipeline validation needs
Fail fast on breaking changes, accommodate additive changes
— missing required columns are errors; new unexpected columns are warnings; this is the safest evolution policy
Read raw data as strings, coerce explicitly
— prevents pandas from making incorrect type inferences that hide upstream data quality problems until much later in the pipeline
